Name: adaptive_entropy_based_quantization
Title: Adaptive Quantization Strategies Based on Entropy Measures for Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: This proposal hypothesizes that incorporating adaptive quantization strategies that utilize real-time entropy measures of model representations can significantly enhance model compression while maintaining task performance, particularly under varying input distributions.
Related Work: Recent works have explored various quantization and pruning methods, focusing on static profiling or fixed strategies (e.g., SQR, PCA Filtering). However, these methods often fail to adapt to input dynamics, resulting in suboptimal performance. This proposal distinguishes itself by advocating for an information-theoretic approach that dynamically adjusts quantization settings based on real-time entropy measures.
Abstract: The rapid deployment of machine learning models necessitates efficient compression techniques that maintain performance while reducing resource demands. This proposal introduces a novel framework for adaptive quantization in neural networks, driven by real-time entropy measures of activations. By leveraging the relationship between entropy and the information content of model representations, we aim to design a quantization strategy that dynamically allocates bit-width based on the task-relevant information observed in the input data. This dynamic quantization approach will enable models to preserve critical information during compression, leading to improved generalization and robustness. We will validate our framework through a series of experiments across various datasets, demonstrating the ability to adaptively optimize quantization on-the-fly while maintaining competitive performance metrics.
Experiments: 1. Entropy Measurement Validation: Measure the entropy of activations across different layers of a neural network during inference using various datasets. 2. Adaptive Quantization Implementation: Implement a quantization strategy that adjusts the bit-width of weights and activations based on the measured entropy. 3. Performance Evaluation: Evaluate model performance on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using metrics such as accuracy and model size. 4. Robustness Testing: Test the adaptive model under different distribution shifts and noisy inputs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The dynamic measurement of entropy may introduce computational overhead. The effectiveness may vary across different model architectures and tasks. Complexity of real-time adjustments could lead to implementation challenges.
